envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly per-unit branded or private-label product sales to retailers, distributors, and OEMs; qualifying outsourced-service revenue is concentrated in recurring contract manufacturing, product configuration, testing, and lifecycle support.
lens.excluded_activities: Major appliances, commercial equipment, import-only brand owners without accountable domestic operations, distributors, captive plants, firms outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of small electric appliances, electric housewares, household fans, vacuums, floor-care machines, air cleaners, portable heaters, food-preparation devices, and similar products that repeatedly supply external private-label, OEM, distributor, and retail customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations across NAICS 335 rather than wage-weighted tasks in small-appliance manufacturers. The range maps exposure to industrial design support, component selection, quoting, demand forecasting, purchasing, production planning, machine-vision inspection, test-data review, compliance documentation, and customer service, while assigning lower exposure to molding, stamping, motor and electronic assembly, wiring, testing, packaging, maintenance, and material handling; broader electrical manufacturing may overweight coil winding and industrial products.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
Vacuums, cooking appliances, fans, floor care, and air cleaners have different electronics, motor, and assembly mixes.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across at least ten domestic small-appliance manufacturers, separating product engineering, sourcing, forecasting, sales, planning, molding and metalwork, electronics, motor assembly, final assembly, testing, quality, packaging, maintenance, compliance, and service.
a.rationale: BLS shows 65,350 electrical and electronic assemblers and 57,660 team assemblers across NAICS 335, demonstrating a large physical core. AI can assist design and information work around assembly, but hands-on production, electrical and thermal testing, packaging, and reliability accountability remain material.

d5.bridge: BLS projects output and employment for all household appliance manufacturing, combining small and major appliances, rather than constant-quality demand for eligible LMM small-appliance contract manufacturers. Major appliances may be more construction-sensitive and domestically produced, while small appliances face faster product cycles and more imports, making the direction of error uncertain.
caveats: BLS output is an aggregate index for small and major appliances and does not isolate eligible operators.
Domestic manufacturing demand can fall even if US consumer unit demand rises through imports.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US unit forecasts by vacuum, fan, air cleaner, portable heat, food preparation, garment care, and floor care, plus installed-base age, replacement cycles, retailer sell-through, returns, imports, channel inventory, and eligible manufacturers' order books.
d5.rationale: BLS projects household-appliance real output rising from 21.9 in 2024 to 24.1 in 2034 while employment falls 7.5%, supporting roughly stable to modestly growing quantity with productivity gains. Population, household formation, replacement, wellness and convenience products support demand; saturation, durability, retailer inventories, imports, and discretionary spending create downside.

caveats: Eligibility depends on whether repeat private-label manufacturing is treated as outsourced service.
The provided n is an ESTIMATE and may include import-led product businesses or firms outside the true band.
e.change_evidence: A complete review of the 22 estimated firms covering normalized EBITDA, contract and private-label revenue, recurring external programs, domestic value added, affiliate sales, brand ownership, owner dependence, certifications, recalls, warranties, and plant condition.
e.rationale: Most firms sell products rather than recurring outsourced services. Domestic private-label and contract manufacturers with repeat external programs can qualify, but branded product companies, importers, captive plants, and commodity assemblers do not. The frozen count of 22 is small and margin-bridged, so even modest classification error materially changes the opportunity.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 335210 ($849,200,248) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 335210 ($4,153,586,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $453,448 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 335210); class <5: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $983,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $192,986 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,689,188 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $920,019 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,125,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $809,456 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,650,409 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,482,010 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,901,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,556,425 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $245,122,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $48,092,985 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 22

caveats: The accountable operator may be an overseas contract manufacturer outside the lens.
Connected features can shift value toward software without eliminating physical appliance demand.
o.change_evidence: Retailer and brand sourcing data measuring shifts to import-only models, direct overseas suppliers, modular commodity hardware, software substitution, and lights-out factories, together with safety certification and product-liability responsibility.
o.rationale: Remaining physical-appliance demand requires an accountable operator to design or configure products, source components, assemble, test electrical and thermal safety, document compliance, manage firmware where relevant, and handle warranties and recalls. Software can enhance or replace narrow functions, but cannot alone provide certified physical appliances.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
A differentiated premium brand can retain much more than a private-label contract assembler.
q.change_evidence: SKU-, retailer-, and OEM-level price-cost-margin bridges before and after automation, including component and freight costs, promotional allowances, chargebacks, private-label resets, returns, warranty costs, volumes, and competitor pricing over three years.
q.rationale: Design, brand, patents, retailer relationships, reliability, and fast product launches can retain some gains. However, retailers and major brands compare global suppliers, negotiate annual cost-downs, demand promotions and allowances, and re-source products; transparent online pricing and low-cost imports pass common productivity improvements through quickly.

caveats: Recall evidence illustrates consequences of quality failures, not an implementation rate.
Import-heavy brands may have data but little domestic labor to automate.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post deployments for AI design support, component substitution, forecasting, scheduling, machine vision, test analysis, predictive maintenance, compliance documentation, and service, reporting labor, yield, returns, incidents, lead time, and overrides.
rho.rationale: CAD, bills of material, retailer forecasts, orders, machine data, inspection images, test results, returns, and service records support AI in engineering assistance, sourcing, planning, quality, and customer service. Implementation is limited by fragmented contract-manufacturer data, safety certification, retailer requirements, short product cycles, cybersecurity in connected products, supply-chain volatility, and recall risk.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. The range discounts intentions for failed sales, internal transfers, closures, product-line deals, and a tiny eligible population; mature ownership may raise exits, while import competition, retail concentration, product obsolescence, and recall exposure can prevent going-concern transfers.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans, not completed transfers.
With few eligible firms, one transaction changes the realized rate sharply.
s5.change_evidence: Ten years of NAICS 335210 closed deals, product-line sales, closures, and owner changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, appliance-industry brokers, SBA loans, UCC records, and state registries, matched to eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: Small product manufacturers can transfer to strategic or private buyers, but the eligible contract-manufacturing subset is likely very small. Retailer concentration, working capital, offshore sourcing, certifications, intellectual property, product liability, and management depth determine financeability.

principal_driver: High-volume digital product, sourcing, forecast, test, and service data can support repeatable AI workflows.
principal_weakness: The eligible domestic contract-manufacturer population is likely very small and exposed to global retail price competition.
